ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried kway teow with beef. FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers stir-fried noodles as a category; no specific code exists for kway teow with beef in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Thai noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried kway teow with beef, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Thai restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pad See Ew / Stir-fried Rice Noodles with Beef — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for stir-fried rice noodle dishes with beef
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried kway teow with beef exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked flat rice noodles (NDB 20113), beef (cooked, lean, NDB 13365), and stir-fried vegetable components, weighted by typical recipe proportions (noodles ~45%, beef ~25%, vegetables ~15%, sauce/oil ~15%). FAO/INFOODS SEA data for similar Thai stir-fried noodle dishes used as cross-reference. Values represent a reasonable per-100g composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles, stir-fried (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried kway teow with beef. GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (cooked, GI ~53, low category), consistent with Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI and GL. The addition of beef protein and fat is expected to lower the effective GI slightly; 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75) / 100 ≈ 34.3, rounded to 34.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per serving) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single serving may contribute approximately 70–84% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from flat rice noodles with a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~53); protein and fat from beef partially attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 65 g of carbohydrate; pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and choosing lean beef can help moderate the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium from sauce-heavy preparation; cholesterol contribution from beef and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the combined sodium and saturated fat burden in this dish and consider requesting less oil and sauce, or limiting frequency of consumption to occasional meals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวผัดเนื้อ, romanized: Kuai Tiao Phat Nuea) is a Thai wok-fried flat rice noodle dish made with wide, silky rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried over high heat with sliced beef, egg, bean sprouts, spring onions, garlic, and a savoury sauce blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce. The dish is cooked in a very hot wok to achieve wok hei — a characteristic smoky, slightly charred aroma and flavour. The noodles are soft and chewy, the beef is tender, and the overall flavour profile is umami-rich, slightly sweet from the dark soy, and savoury. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Sanook Kitchen and is a popular lunch and dinner option. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 518 kcal, 30 g protein, 17 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~1680 mg per serving) due to the sauce components, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category), though the carbohydrate load per serving is substantial. The dish contains gluten (soy sauce), egg, and shellfish derivatives (oyster sauce) and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried kway teow with beef was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for cooked flat rice noodles and lean cooked beef, weighted by typical recipe proportions, with FAO/INFOODS SEA used as a cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food name '716D Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef and' appears to be a truncated menu item code from field survey data (Sanook Kitchen - Funan). The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef'. The '716D' prefix is a restaurant menu code and has been preserved in aliases. 2. The word 'and' at the end of the original name suggests the full dish name may include an additional ingredient (e.g., 'with Beef and Egg' or 'with Beef and Vegetables'); however, no confirmed full name was available from the source. The record has been prepared based on the standard Thai stir-fried kway teow with beef formulation. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data for this dish type. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the reference sources used. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the reference sources used. 6. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce is a common ingredient and halal certification of the specific restaurant preparation cannot be confirmed from available data. 7. GI of 53 is a conservative analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice noodles; the actual GI of this specific dish may be lower due to protein and fat contributions from beef and egg. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (53 × 64.75 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.3, reported as 34.2.
